preFix.title: Routing a rebate with a router
preFix.subtitle: Cut a precise stepped shoulder for glass or panel fitting
preFix.excerpt: How to cut a rebate using a plunge or fixed-base router: selecting the right bit, setting the fence and depth, making multiple light passes to the finished depth, and squaring the corners with a chisel where needed.
preFix.sourceNotes: Newey and Drage Practical Carpentry and Joinery. Collins Complete DIY Manual. Cassell's Cyclopaedia of Mechanics.

content.text: A
rebate

cut with a router produces sharp, clean shoulders at the correct depth. The key is multiple light passes rather than cutting to full depth in one go. A shallow first pass registers the cut cleanly; the second or third pass reaches full depth without the router grabbing.
When to use it

Routing a rebate suits glass panel fitting in picture frames and cabinet doors. It also works for the edge return on a box lid or the joint that receives a panel back on a cabinet carcass. On timber wider than 75 mm, a router is far faster than a rebate plane.
What you will need
A plunge or fixed-base router with a
straight bit
sized to the rebate width, or a bearing-guided rebate bit. The router's side fence. A pair of G-cramps. A chisel set and sandpaper on a sanding block to clean the rebate floor.
Step 1, mark and set up

Mark the rebate width and depth on the workpiece end grain with the combination square. Fit the straight bit and set the fence so the bit aligns with the inner edge of the rebate. Set the depth stop to 3 mm for the first pass. Clamp the workpiece securely to the bench; a rebate cut requires both hands on the router.
Step 2, make multiple passes to depth

Run the router along the length of the workpiece, keeping the fence pressed firmly against the edge throughout. Feed the router from left to right when viewed from above so the bit rotation works against the fence, pulling into the cut rather than away from it. After the first pass, increase the depth by 3 to 4 mm and make a second pass. Continue until the final depth is reached.
Step 3, square the corners by hand

The router leaves rounded ends at the start and stop of each pass because the bit has a radius. Square these with a sharp chisel: hold the chisel vertical and pare across the grain, using the chisel registration on the rebate floor. One or two firm cuts is usually enough to bring the corner to a right angle.
What it looks like when it is right

The rebate floor is smooth and flat. The shoulder is a clean right angle at the correct depth. A glass panel or timber panel laid in the rebate sits flat without rocking or binding. If the floor is rippled or stepped, the passes were too deep; sand the floor smooth with 120-grit on a block.
Common mistakes

Feeding the router in the wrong direction. Feed from left to right (climb cut) only for a final light finish pass; feeding right to left for the full cut causes the router to tear out and pull sideways.
Lifting the router at the end of the pass before switching it off. Always complete the full pass, switch off, and wait for the bit to stop before lifting clear.
